name: Nerva, El Madroño,
name_original: Nerva, El Madroño, Berrocal – ruta circular
description: This difficult 36.3-mile (58.5 km) road cycling route from Nerva offers 3734 feet (1138 metres) of climbing through Andalusian landscapes.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Nerva, located in the province of Huelva, Spain, traverse a landscape characterized by significant elevation changes and historical mining areas. The terrain features numerous hills and valleys, providing challenging climbs and descents for road cyclists. This region offers a network of roads that wind through both natural and industrial heritage sites, with routes often featuring substantial elevation gain. The area's geology contributes to varied road surfaces and scenic vistas. Best road cycling routes around Nerva The most popular road cycling route is Old Zarandas-Naya Station – Riotinto Mines loop from Zarandas-Naya, a 60.4 miles (97.3 km) trail that takes 5 hours 6 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the historic mining landscapes of the region. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Old Zarandas-Naya Station – Riotinto Mining Museum loop from Zarandas-Naya, a difficult 59.6 miles (95.9 km) path. This route offers sustained climbing and views of the mining museum area. Local road cyclists also love the Nerva, El Madroño,, a 36.3 miles (58.5 km) trail leading through hilly terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 14 minutes. Road cycling around Nerva is defined by mining landscapes, rolling hills, and varied elevation. The network offers options for moderate to difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Nerva's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Nerva?
A: Road cycling around Nerva is characterized by significant elevation changes, offering challenging climbs and descents. The routes traverse a landscape of rolling hills and valleys, often featuring substantial elevation gain. You'll encounter varied road surfaces as you explore both natural and historical mining areas.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Nerva?
A: The road cycling routes around Nerva are generally geared towards moderate to difficult ability levels due to the hilly terrain and elevation changes. There are no easy routes listed in the area, with 3 routes rated moderate and 12 rated difficult.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I can see while cycling in Nerva?
A: The Nerva region is rich in natural and industrial heritage. Along your rides, you might encounter significant sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4402997" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Río Tinto&lt;/a&gt;, the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5287308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corta Atalaya Mine&lt;/a&gt;, or the geological formation of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5300427" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña del Hierro&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6268303" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gossán Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; also offers scenic views.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Nerva?
A: Yes, there are circular road cycling routes available. For example, you can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991344075/nerva-minas-de-riotinto-zalamea-la-real-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nerva, Minas de Riotinto, Zalamea La Real – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which covers approximately 53.5 km with significant elevation.

Q: What is a challenging road cycling route in the Nerva area?
A: For a challenging experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31857282" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Zarandas-Naya Station – Riotinto Mines loop from Zarandas-Naya&lt;/a&gt;. This difficult route spans about 97.3 km and involves over 1500 meters of elevation gain, taking around 5 hours to complete.

Q: Can I find routes that combine cycling with historical exploration in Nerva?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in Nerva wind through historical mining landscapes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20122970" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Zarandas-Naya Station – Riotinto Mining Museum loop from Zarandas-Naya&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering sustained climbing and views of the mining museum area over a distance of nearly 96 km.

Q: What are the typical durations for road cycling routes around Nerva?
A: The durations for road cycling routes in Nerva vary depending on the distance and elevation. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991622435/nerva-el-madrono" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nerva, El Madroño,&lt;/a&gt; route typically takes about 3 hours and 14 minutes for its 58.5 km, while longer, more difficult routes can take over 5 hours.

Q: Are there any viewpoints along the road cycling routes in Nerva?
A: Yes, the hilly terrain of Nerva offers several scenic viewpoints. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20122208" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Berrocal Viewpoint – El Madroño loop from Nerva&lt;/a&gt; is a route that specifically highlights a viewpoint, providing panoramic vistas of the surrounding landscape.

Q: What kind of attractions can I visit near the cycling routes in Nerva?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can visit man-made attractions that reflect the region's history. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5082202" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Touristic Railway for Rio Tinto Mines&lt;/a&gt; offers a unique historical experience, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5345695" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cachán Bridge&lt;/a&gt; is another point of interest.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Nerva area?
A: There are 15 road cycling routes documented around Nerva, offering a variety of options for cyclists looking to explore the region's unique landscape.

Q: What makes road cycling in Nerva unique?
A: Road cycling in Nerva is unique due to its combination of challenging, hilly terrain and its rich industrial heritage. Cyclists can experience significant elevation changes while passing through historical mining landscapes and natural features, offering a distinct blend of physical challenge and cultural exploration.
tips: This small but picturesque town in the interior of Seville borders the province of Huelva to the west and you can get here following some paths next to the Mining Basin. Among its places of interest, the Church of San Blas and the beautiful Hermitage next to the Town Hall stand out.
From the bridge over the Gallego stream there is a slight ascent, smooth except for a curved section and with a slope of almost 8%. The landscape has recovered well after the 2004 fire.
Obligatory stopping point if you do the road. There are incredible views even of the Rio Tinto mining railway.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Nerva, El Madroño, Berrocal circular route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as difficult for racebiking. It covers approximately 58.5 km with a significant elevation gain of about 1,138 meters. Expect substantial climbs and descents, requiring good to very good fitness.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: The terrain features varied landscapes, including rolling hills and mountainous areas typical of the Huelva province. While the route is primarily designed for racebikes, implying mostly paved roads, some sections in the broader region can include unpaved segments. Be prepared for ascents, such as one near El Madroño that is 3.5 km long with an average gradient of 5.1%.

faqs.question: What natural features or landmarks will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience amazing natural diversity, including rolling sierras and areas with beautiful holm oak groves. Key highlights include passing through the settlement of El Madroño, crossing the Arroyo Gallego, and enjoying views from the El Berrocal Viewpoint. The route offers sweeping views of valleys and breathtaking scenery.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, Nerva is a common starting point for circular routes in the area. You can typically find parking within the towns along the route, such as Nerva, El Madroño, or Berrocal. Look for public parking spaces in these municipalities.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The mild climate of Andalusia makes this route particularly enjoyable from February to May and again from October to November. These periods offer comfortable temperatures for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll encounter short sections of the Bajada Los Herreros, Camino Carril, Media Luna (Valdejulián), Camino forestal los Herreros, and the Vía Verde del Río Tinto.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating, significant elevation gain, and distance of nearly 60 km, this route is not recommended for beginners or young families. It requires a good level of fitness and experience with racebiking on challenging terrain.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This is a racebike route primarily on public roads and through towns. While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on the route itself, it's generally not suitable for cycling with dogs due to the speed, distance, and road conditions. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured and consider shorter, more dog-friendly trails.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and paths connecting the towns of Nerva, El Madroño, and Berrocal.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through several towns like Nerva, El Madroño, and Berrocal, you will find opportunities for cafes, restaurants, and shops to refuel. These towns offer a chance to experience local gastronomy, including Iberian ham and seafood.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this racebike route?
faqs.answer: On average, completing this 58.5 km racebike route takes approximately 3 hours and 15 minutes (11,670 seconds) of active cycling time. This duration can vary based on your fitness level, speed, and any stops you make.
